title: How much does electric underfloor heating cost to run?
description: Electric underfloor heating costs roughly 8 to 12p per square metre a day, so about £10 a month for a bathroom. Real UK figures, tables and saving tips.
blurb: Real per-room running costs for electric underfloor heating, why bathrooms suit it, and where it gets expensive fast.
lede: Electric underfloor heating has a reputation problem. Ask one neighbour and it is a cheap luxury that costs pennies; ask another and it is the reason their winter bill doubled. Both can be right, because the running cost depends almost entirely on how much floor you heat and for how long. A few square metres of warm tile in a bathroom genuinely does cost pennies a day. The same heating mat stretched across a whole ground floor, running on standard-rate electricity, is one of the most expensive ways to heat a home in Britain. This guide puts real numbers on both ends of that scale so you can see exactly where your plans would land.
short_answer: A typical bathroom system, with 3 to 4 square metres of 150 W per square metre matting, costs roughly 30 to 40p a day in winter, or about £10 a month at the current cap rate of 26.11p per kWh. As a rule of thumb, budget 8 to 12p per square metre of heated floor per day during the heating season. Bathroom-sized systems are cheap comfort; heating whole rooms or a whole house this way costs three times what mains gas would for the same warmth.

sections.h2: What electric underfloor heating actually draws
sections.html:
Electric underfloor heating is rated in watts per square metre. The common options are 100 W/m² for well-insulated rooms, 150 W/m² as the standard choice, and 200 W/m² for cold or fast-warming spaces such as conservatories. Multiply the rating by the heated area and you get the maximum draw. A 3.5 m² bathroom mat at 150 W/m² pulls 525 W flat out, about half a kettle.

Two things stop the real cost being as scary as that sounds.

  • You never heat the whole room. Mats go in the open floor area only, not under the bath, toilet, shower tray or kitchen units. The heated area is usually 50 to 80 per cent of the room size, so a "4 metre bathroom" might carry just 2 to 2.5 m² of mat.
  • The thermostat cycles the power. The system runs at full draw while the floor warms up, then switches on and off to hold temperature. Once a tiled floor over insulation board is up to heat, the element is typically live for only 30 to 50 per cent of the time. Six hours of scheduled heating might cost the same as two to three hours at full power.

That is why the honest unit for budgeting is full-power-equivalent hours. For a bathroom used morning and evening, 2 to 3 full-power-equivalent hours a day in winter is realistic. A living room kept warm all evening needs more like 4 to 5.

The table below uses the standard 150 W/m² rating (200 W/m² for the conservatory), realistic heated areas, and winter usage expressed as full-power-equivalent hours. Costs are at the current price cap unit rate.

RoomHeated areaFull-power drawWinter use per dayCost per dayCost per month
Ensuite2 m²300 W0.6 kWh16p£4.70
Family bathroom3.5 m²525 W1.3 kWh34p£10.30
Kitchen8 m²1,200 W3.6 kWh94p£28.20
Living room15 m²2,250 W9 kWh£2.35£70.50
Conservatory (200 W/m²)12 m²2,400 W12 kWh£3.13£94.00

At 26.11p per kWh, Ofgem price cap July to September 2026, checked 10 June 2026. Winter use shown as full-power-equivalent consumption; your thermostat schedule may differ.

Over a heating season of roughly seven months, with shoulder months lighter than January, a family bathroom comes out at £60 to £90 a year. The living room example lands closer to £400 a year, and that is the point where most people should pause and compare alternatives.

sections.h2: Why bathrooms make sense and whole houses usually don't
sections.html:
The economics hinge on one comparison: electricity at 26.11p per kWh against mains gas at 7.33p per kWh. Every electric heater converts 100 per cent of what it draws into heat, so there is no clever efficiency gap to exploit. A condensing gas boiler delivers heat at roughly 8p per kWh after boiler losses, which means electric underfloor heating costs a bit over three times as much per unit of warmth as gas central heating.

For a bathroom, that multiple barely matters. You are buying a small amount of targeted comfort, perhaps £70 a year, in the one room where warm feet on cold tile feel worth every penny. It also lets you keep the radiator off or downsized in that room, and it pairs naturally with a modest electric towel rail on the same schedule.

For a whole house, the multiple is brutal. A home needing 10,000 kWh of heat a year would pay roughly £800 on gas but about £2,600 on standard-rate electric underfloor heating. If you are weighing underfloor heating as a main heating system rather than a comfort add-on, the water-based version fed by a boiler or heat pump is a different and much stronger proposition; our guide to underfloor heating versus radiators covers that comparison properly.

The middle cases, kitchens and single living rooms, depend on your insulation and habits. A well-insulated kitchen where the mat takes the morning chill off for an hour is fine. The same kitchen heated to 21°C from 6am to 10pm through January is not.

sections.h2: How to work out your own figure
sections.html:
You can estimate any system in four steps, the same method we use across our appliance running cost guides.

  • Heated area in m² times the mat rating in W/m², divided by 1,000, gives the full-power draw in kW.
  • Multiply by your full-power-equivalent hours per day. Use 2 to 3 for a bathroom on a morning and evening schedule, 4 to 5 for an evening living room, more for poorly insulated rooms.
  • Multiply by your unit rate from your bill, or 26.11p if you are on the capped standard variable rate.
  • Multiply by 30 for a monthly figure, remembering usage drops sharply outside December to February.

Worked example: 5 m² of 150 W/m² mat is 0.75 kW. At 2.5 hours a day that is 1.875 kWh, which costs 49p a day or about £14.70 a month at the cap rate.

What the spec sheet won't tell you

Electric underfloor heating carries no efficiency rating to compare, because every resistance heater is equally efficient at the point of use. That is different from fridges or washing machines, where energy labels let you compare models. With underfloor heating, the saving comes from insulation, control and floor build-up, not from the brand of cable.

One small constant cost worth knowing about: the thermostat itself draws a watt or two around the clock, which adds £3 to £5 a year. It is a classic example of the quiet background drain covered in our guide to standby power, and not a reason to isolate the circuit in summer unless you prefer to.

sections.h2: Seven ways to cut the running cost
sections.html:
• Fit insulation boards under the mat. On a bare concrete or screed subfloor, 6 to 10 mm of insulation board is the single biggest saving available. Without it, a large share of your heat soaks downwards into the slab; manufacturers commonly quote warm-up energy cut by around half, and heat-up times fall from over an hour to 15 to 20 minutes.
  • Control floor temperature, not air temperature. Use the floor sensor and aim for a comfortable 25 to 27°C floor rather than asking a bathroom mat to heat the whole room's air. Wood and laminate floors usually have a 27°C limit anyway.
  • Schedule tightly around real use. With insulation board the floor warms in minutes, so a bathroom needs two short windows a day, not all-day running. A thermostat with a setback temperature, holding perhaps 16°C between comfort periods, beats fully off-then-on in heavyweight floors.
  • Choose conductive flooring. Tile and stone pass heat readily, so the floor feels warm at lower running temperatures. Thick carpet and underlay insulate you from your own heating; most systems set a combined limit of about 1.5 to 2.5 tog above the element.
  • Drop the thermostat a degree. As with all heating, each degree off the target trims roughly 6 to 10 per cent from consumption.
  • Use cheap-rate electricity if your system stores heat. Mats directly under tile have almost no thermal mass, but cables buried in a thick screed act like a storage heater and can charge on an off-peak overnight rate, releasing heat through the morning.
  • Don't use it to fight a cold room. If the room leaks heat through an uninsulated floor, single glazing or draughts, fix those first. Underfloor heating amplifies the cost of a leaky room because the floor is the first thing losing heat downwards.

Electric underfloor heating costs roughly 8 to 12p per square metre of heated floor per day in winter at the current 26.11p per kWh cap rate. For a bathroom that means around £10 a month in the cold months and £60 to £90 a year, a small price for a real comfort upgrade and cheap enough that it rarely deserves agonising over. Scale the same technology up to whole rooms or a whole home on standard-rate electricity and the arithmetic turns against you fast, at roughly three times the cost of gas for the same warmth. Keep it small, put insulation board underneath, run it on a tight schedule with the floor sensor in charge, and it earns its place. Plan anything bigger and you should be pricing wet underfloor heating, a heat pump or better insulation before you order another roll of mat.

faq.q: Is electric underfloor heating expensive to run?
faq.a: Per unit of heat it costs over three times as much as mains gas, but the systems people actually fit are small. A bathroom installation costs roughly 30 to 40p a day in winter at the July to September 2026 cap rate of 26.11p per kWh. It only becomes expensive when used as the main heating for large rooms or a whole house.

faq.q: How much does underfloor heating cost per month in a bathroom?
faq.a: About £10 a month in winter for a typical family bathroom with 3 to 4 square metres of 150 W per square metre matting, run morning and evening. A small ensuite is nearer £5. Over a full year, expect £60 to £90 because spring and autumn use is much lighter.

faq.q: Is it cheaper to leave electric underfloor heating on all day?
faq.a: Not for thin mats under tile, which warm up in 15 to 20 minutes over insulation board, so short scheduled windows are cheapest. Cables buried in a thick screed are slower to respond, and there a setback temperature between comfort periods usually beats switching fully off and reheating from cold.

faq.q: Does electric underfloor heating use more electricity than an electric radiator?
faq.a: For the same room kept at the same temperature, the energy use is broadly similar because all electric resistance heating is 100 per cent efficient. Underfloor heating can come out slightly ahead in practice because the warm floor lets people feel comfortable at a lower air temperature, but it will not slash the bill on its own.

faq.q: Can I run electric underfloor heating on an off-peak tariff like Economy 7?
faq.a: Only usefully if the heating cables are buried in a thick screed, which stores overnight cheap-rate heat and releases it through the day like a storage heater. Thin mats directly under tile have almost no thermal mass, so off-peak charging does little for them because the heat is gone within the hour.
